retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The efficacy of fingolimod in treating RRMS has been demonstrated in 3 separate double-blind, randomized, phase III clinical trials. In the placebo-controlled FREEDOMS trial (FTY720 Research Evaluating Effects of Daily Oral Therapy in Multiple Sclerosis), fingolimod demonstrated improved relapse rate, decreased disease disability progression, reduced number of active or enlarging magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) lesions, and brain atrophy over 2 years. The FREEDOMS II trial evaluated fingolimod's safety profile and found that daily administration of 0.5 mg had a favorable benefit-risk ratio when treating RRMS.

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The TRANSFORMS trial (Trial Assessing Injectable Interferon versus FTY720 Oral in Relapsing-Remitting Multiple Sclerosis) demonstrated the superiority of fingolimod therapy in reducing disease activity and relapses in RRMS when compared to interferon β-1a. [2] [4] [5] Studies in specific patient populations, including the IRST LATAM study, analyzed fingolimod's effect on Latin American patients with diagnosed multiple sclerosis. The medication's pharmacodynamics and safety profile did not differ among these patients. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Transplantation Rejection(Archived) -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: FTY720 (fingolimod) is a novel immunosuppressive agent investigated over the past few decades. FTY has proven highly effective in prolonging graft survival in preclinical models of transplantation and multiple sclerosis. Unlike traditional immunosuppressants, FTY720 is an immunomodulator with a unique mechanism of action, classified as a sphingosine 1-phosphate receptor (S1P-R) modulator. [8] [9] In laboratory animals, activating S1P receptors with fingolimod triggers anti-inflammatory and anti-apoptotic pathways, enhancing myocardial salvage by reducing cardiac fibrosis. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Fingolimod is the first oral disease-modifying agent approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS). [1] This drug is also indicated for active secondary progressive disease with superimposed relapse and for treating clinically isolated syndromes. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fingolimod is a nonselective functional antagonist of sphingosine-1-phosphate receptors (S1PRs). [1] Since the emergence of fingolimod in 2010 as the first medication of this class, similar medications with higher receptor specificity have been synthesized and FDA-approved, such as siponimod (2019), ozanimod (2020), and ponesimod (2021). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Fingolimod is the first oral disease-modifying agent approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating multiple sclerosis. The drug effectively manages relapsing and remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS), clinically isolated syndromes, and active secondary progressive disease. The primary function of fingolimod is to inhibit lymphocyte migration into peripheral circulation by antagonizing sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor-1 (S1PR1) functions, thereby reducing the body's inflammatory response. Additionally, fingolimod modulates various immune system activities, including T-cell central and peripheral tolerance, autoreactivity, T-cell differentiation, and regulation of the innate immune system. However, concerns exist regarding fingolimod's impact on the cardiovascular system, particularly cardiac chronotropy, which may elevate mortality risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy considerations: Fingolimod has a low molecular weight, allowing it to cross the placental membrane easily. [33] Preclinical data suggest that sphingosine receptors might help regulate placenta and embryonic angiogenesis. [33] [34] Therefore, the FDA recommends that all women of childbearing age use at least two different forms of contraception while taking fingolimod and for 2 months after discontinuing the medication. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Glatiramer -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Michael Sela, Ruth Arnon, and Dvora Teitelbaum at the Weizmann Institute of Science in Rehovot, Israel, originally discovered the drug in the 1960s as they attempted to produce a synthetic antigen capable of inducing experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis. Experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis is the animal model of autoimmune inflammatory CNS disorders used by researchers to advance understanding of such neurological conditions. Surprisingly, they noted that the copolymer they created, Cop-1, reduced the incidence of encephalitis and the severity of histological lesions. After testing Cop-1 on various species and achieving similar results, clinical evaluation of human subjects began in 1977. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Natalizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Natalizumab is an FDA-approved monoclonal antibody for treating multiple sclerosis and Crohn disease (CD). [1] The drug was initially approved to treat multiple sclerosis in 2004, but after reported mortality cases due to progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy associated with natalizumab treatment, the FDA removed the drug from the market. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: Fingolimod formulations are available as oral capsules in strengths of 0.25 mg and 0.5 mg. Food consumption does not affect drug administration. The recommended dosage for adults with a body weight of less than or equal to 40 kg (88 lb) is a 0.5 mg tablet once daily. For pediatric patients aged 10 or older with a body weight of less than 40 kg (88 lb), the recommended fingolimod dosage is a 0.25 mg tablet once daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Glatiramer -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Glatiramer acetate is an immune-modulating medication that is FDA-approved to treat relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS), clinically isolated syndrome (CIS), and active secondary progressive disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Natalizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Natalizumab has been shown to slow down the progression of symptoms during a flare-up in individuals with multiple sclerosis and decrease the rate of relapse in those with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis. In a randomized, placebo-controlled study, 77% of patients were relapse-free at the end of 1 year compared to 56% of those on placebo. The 2-year relapse-free rate was 67% with natalizumab versus 41% with placebo. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Natalizumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In 2006, the FDA reintroduced the drug when multiple protests by those with multiple sclerosis advocated for being able to use natalizumab in conjunction with the establishment of an advisory committee that would monitor patients on natalizumab. All patients receiving treatment with natalizumab are part of a TOUCH Prescribing Program database. A facility is granted permission by the United States Federal Drug Administration to administer natalizumab and ensure that extensive monitoring data and follow-up regarding the patient's experience with natalizumab are collected. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Fingolimod -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Fingolimod has an oral bioavailability of 93%, unaffected by food intake. The drug achieves a steady state within 8 to 36 hours after administration, with a half-life ranging from 6 to 9 days. [4] Distribution: Approximately 86% of fingolimod is distributed via red blood cells, with a volume of distribution of approximately 1200 ± 260 L.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Transplantation Rejection(Archived) -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: ASP0028, an S1P1/S1P5-selective agonist with comparable efficacy to FTY720 and a broader safety profile, demonstrated both efficacy and safety when coadministered with a suboptimal dose of tacrolimus in a monkey renal transplantation model. ASP0028 administration remarkably reduced peripheral lymphocytes, including subsets of CD4+ or CD8+ naive and central memory cells, CD4+ Treg cells, and B cells, while not affecting CD4+ or CD8+ effector memory cells and NK cells. These findings suggest that ASP0028, in combination with tacrolimus, effectively prolongs renal allograft survival in primates. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: class includes methylfenidát (a centrally acting sympathomimetic used for ADHD and nootropic purposes), atomoxetine, modafinil and a subset termed “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” that contains acylcarnitine, adrafinil (an adrenergic‑α agonist), cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline, deanol (often formulated with hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL in topical preparations), fipexide, nizofenone, pipradrol, prolintane, temgicoluril, and related agents such as mebikar, which is linked to temgicoluril. Methylfenidát, pipradrol and prolintane share chemical or therapeutic overlap with antidepressants such as klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram and tofisofam. Riluzole serves as a parent compound for the drug group “primarily affecting the central nervous system”; together with deutetrabenazine and tetrabenazine, it resides in the ATC class “Other nervous system drugs” and is used for movement disorders and neuroprotection. Cyclobenzaprine, although chemically related to tricyclic antidepressants,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: Within mental health behavior, antidepressants represent a broad pharmacologic group that includes several agents and related compounds. Lithium is listed as a child of the antidepressant category, reflecting its use as an adjunct mood stabilizer in depressive disorders. Lamotrigine, although primarily an anticonvulsant, is associated with the therapeutic class of antidepressants in some classifications, indicating its role in mood stabilization. Casopitant and sulpiride are identified as types of antidepressants, although they are more commonly known as an NK1‑receptor antagonist and an atypical antipsychotic, respectively, highlighting the overlapping pharmacologic profiles among psychotropic drugs. Bupropion and its salt forms, bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride, are recognized as child agents of the antidepressant group and are assigned specific therapeutic classes. However, bupropion and its salt forms are contraindicated in patients with متلازمة الانسحاب (withdrawal syndrome), underscoring the need for caution when prescribing these agents to individuals at risk of withdrawal. Additional compounds classified under the antidepressant umbrella include klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, and tofisopam, each representing distinct chemical entities with potential antidepressant activity. Notably, tofisopam is reported to may treat متلازمة الانسحاب, suggesting a therapeutic option for managing withdrawal symptoms, which contrasts with the contraindication of bupropion in the same condition. Overall, these relationships illustrate the complex taxonomy of antidepressant‑related drugs, the importance of recognizing contraindications such as withdrawal syndrome, and the potential for certain agents like tofisopam to be employed in its treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1045
retrieved_docs.content: Bupropion is identified as a substance used for smoking cessation (látky pro odvykání kouření) and is also classified within the therapeutic class of antidepressants. Both the hydrobromide and hydrochloride salt forms of bupropion share this antidepressant classification and are contraindicated in patients with withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الانسحاب). Within the broader category of antidepressants, a diverse group of agents is included: lithium, casopitant, esketamine, sulpirid, femoxetine, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, and tofisopam. Tofisopam, while an antidepressant, is noted to have the potential to treat withdrawal syndrome, contrasting with the contraindication of bupropion and its salts for the same condition. The concept of mental health behavior is linked hierarchically to antidepressants, indicating that antidepressant therapy is a foundational element in addressing mental health behavioral issues. This integrated view highlights the multiple pharmacologic options within the antidepressant class, their specific therapeutic roles, and important safety considerations such as contraindications and potential therapeutic benefits for withdrawal-related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 592
retrieved_docs.content: Bupropion is classified as a substance for smoking cessation (látky pro odvykání kouření) and is also listed as a child of the antidepressant class. Both bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride share the therapeutic class of antidepressants. Within the broad antidepressant category, numerous agents are identified, including casopitant, esketamine, sulpirid, femoxetine, fluoxetin, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, and tofisopam, each described as a type or child of antidepressants. Lithium is also noted as a child of antidepressants, although it is traditionally a mood stabilizer. The antidepressant class is linked to the concept of mental health behavior as a parent category. Bupropion and its salt forms are contraindicated in patients with withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الانسحاب), indicating they should not be used in that context. Conversely, tofisopam is indicated as a possible treatment for withdrawal syndrome, suggesting a therapeutic role for that condition. This network of relationships highlights the classification of various psychotropic agents, their therapeutic uses, and specific safety considerations relevant to patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “látky ovlivňující centrální nervový systém” (substances affecting the central nervous system) is a child of the higher‑level concept “Psychoactive substance” and is divided into neuroprotective substances (e.g., almotriptan malate, fenfluramin‑hydrochlorid, alfentanil, sodium phenylbutyrate, ziconotide) and psychotropic drugs. Antidepressants occupy both the CNS‑active and psychotropic drug hierarchies, are inversely related to the class “psychoanaleptics,” and are linked to the disorder “Antidepressant drug adverse reaction,” a disorder caused by psychoactive substances. Representative antidepressant agents include lithium, desvenlafaxine succinate, trazodone, duloxetinum hydrochloride, esketamine, sulpiride, atomoxetine, bupropion, deanol, femoxetine, fipexide, klorgylin, moklobemide, nialamide, rolipram, tofisopam, the selective serotonin re‑uptake inhibitors (SSRIs) and their class, as well as methylfenidát, pipradrol and prolintane. Aniracetam is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: catalogued in ATC are acylcarnitine, adrafinil (an adrenergic α‑receptor agonist), cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline, fipexide, pipradrol, prolintane, and temgicoluril (which is linked to mebikar). In withdrawal management, bupropion and its salts are contraindicated in patients with withdrawal syndrome, whereas tofisopam may be employed to treat such syndrome; detoxification is directly related to withdrawal syndrome. This integrated network illustrates the overlapping classifications among antidepressants, stimulants, and nootropics, highlights important drug‑drug contraindications, and clarifies the placement of these agents within the larger pharmacological taxonomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: as a nootropic, highlighting its cognitive‑enhancing and mood‑modulating properties. Bupropion is recognized as a smoking‑cessation agent; its salt forms (bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride) are placed in the antidepressant class. Tricyclic antidepressants form a distinct subclass of antidepressants and are contraindicated with the anti‑arrhythmic dronedarone hydrochloride. Antidepressants belong to the broader psychoanaleptic group and include citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetinum hydrochloride, esketamine, indeloxazine, atomoxetine, deanol, fipexide, progabide, sidnocarb, sulpiride, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, sertraline, tofisopam and other tricyclic agents; trazodone is noted as a related parent compound and lithium is used adjunctively as a mood‑stabilizing agent. Central‑nervous‑system stimulants such as methylfenidát, pipradrol and prolintane are linked to antidepressant therapy, reflecting off‑label use for mood elevation. Centrally

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 111
retrieved_docs.content: Psychostimulants form a subclass of the broader psychoanaleptic (PSYCHOANALEPTICS) category. The ATC group “OTHER NERVOUS SYSTEM DRUGS” includes riluzole, deutetrabenazine and tetrabenazine and is defined as a type of parasympathomimetic; riluzole serves as a parent compound for drug groups primarily affecting the CNS. The ATC subgroup “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” comprises acylcarnitine, cytidine diphosphate choline, deanol, fipexide, pipradrol, prolintane and temgicoluril. Deanol is explicitly distinguished from parasympathomimetics and may be formulated with the inactive ingredient hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL topical cream. These hierarchical classifications and interaction/contraindication relationships illustrate how CNS‑active agents—including anticonvulsants, tranquilizers, psychostimulants, nootropics, antidepressants, anti‑addiction drugs and related ATC groups—are organized, share pharmacologic properties, and require careful consideration for safe prescribing and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 365
retrieved_docs.content: The Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) class “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” encompasses a diverse group of agents that are used for cognitive enhancement and stimulant effects. Included in this category are the racetam nootropic aniracetam, the metabolic compound acylcarnitine, the choline precursor cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline (CDP‑choline), the psychostimulant fipexide, and the additional substances nizofenone, pipradrol, prolintane, and temgicoluril. Aniracetam is specifically identified as a member of the broader class of nootropic substances (“nootropní látky”), underscoring its role in improving memory and learning. By grouping these compounds together, the ATC classification highlights their shared therapeutic focus on enhancing mental function and providing stimulant activity, which is valuable for clinicians when selecting agents for conditions such as cognitive decline, attention deficits, or fatigue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1547
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyzine hydrochloride and tofisopam are identified as therapeutic options that may be used to treat متلازمة الانسحاب (withdrawal syndrome), offering clinicians potential pharmacologic strategies for managing the symptoms of this condition. In contrast, both bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride are contraindicated in patients with متلازمة الانسحاب, indicating that these formulations of bupropion should be avoided in the presence of withdrawal syndrome to prevent adverse effects. This distinction guides appropriate drug selection, promoting the use of hydroxyzine hydrochloride or tofisopam for treatment while excluding bupropion products in affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: Aniracetam is listed in the ATC subgroup “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” and is simultaneously classified as an antidepressant and a nootropic substance. Bupropion bridges several therapeutic domains as an antidepressant, a smoking‑cessation agent, and a member of the “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” group; its salt forms (bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride) are also assigned to the antidepressant class. Within the mental‑health hierarchy, the category “Mental health.behavior” sits above antidepressants, which themselves are a subcategory of the broader class of psycho‑analeptics. The antidepressant class encompasses a diverse array of agents, including citalopram, lithium (used adjunctively in depression), trazodone (a prototypical parent), casopitant, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride, esketamine, sulpiride (an antipsychotic with overlapping use), atomoxetine, femoxetine, fipexide, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, tofisofam,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” comprises agents such as aniracetam, acylcarnitine, adrafinil (a pro‑drug of modafinil and α‑adrenergic agonist), cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline, fipexide, nizofenone, oxiracetam, pipradrol, prolintane, temgicoluril (structurally related to mebikar) and others, classified as psychoanaleptics used for ADHD and cognitive enhancement. Within this group, centrally acting sympathomimetics—including atomoxetine, methamfetamin, methylfenidát and modafinil—are employed for the same indications. Antidepressants form a distinct subclass of psychoanaleptics and are therapeutically linked to the sodium‑valproate salt class; representative members include citalopram, lithium, bupropion (and its hydrobromide and hydrochloride salts, also a smoking‑cessation agent), desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride, esketamine, sidnocarb, sulpiride, atomoxetine, fipexide, iproniazid (also listed under antituberculotics for its historic MAOI activity), klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolapram, tofisopam, methylfenidát, pipradrol and prolintane, with trazodone serving as the prototypical parent concept. Important safety interactions are noted: citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride and trazodone are contraindicated with the oxazolidinone antibiotic linezolid because of serotonin‑syndrome risk; citalopram use may be complicated by alcohol dependence; and bupropion and its salts are contraindicated in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome, underscoring the need to evaluate dependence‑related conditions before prescribing. Tofisopam is specifically indicated for the treatment of withdrawal syndrome, highlighting its role in managing substance‑discontinuation symptoms. The trade name Antituberkulotika refers to an isoniazid 100 mg/mL injectable solution used for Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection, illustrating the therapeutic application of antitubercular agents such as iproniazid within this context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: trazodone. Hydroxyzine hydrochloride and tofisopam have been identified as potentially useful for treating withdrawal syndrome, whereas bupropion and its salt forms are contraindicated in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome. Chinolony is chemically related to karteolol as its parent structure and is mapped to L‑701324 and indacaterol, with downstream mapping to nequinate, illustrating a network of structural analogues across therapeutic classes. Within the ATC category “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” (a subclass of psychostimulants) the compounds acylcarnitine, cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline, deanol, fipexide, nizofenone, pipradrol, prolintane and temgicoluril are listed; mebikar is linked pharmacologically or classificatorily to temgicoluril. Formulations of deanol may contain hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL topical cream as an inactive ingredient. Overall, the community’s knowledge emphasizes extensive interconnections among anticonvulsants, tranquilizers, antidepressants,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 468
retrieved_docs.content: Antidepressants are a therapeutic class within the broader domain of mental health behavior. Several specific agents are classified as antidepressants, including casopitant, esketamine, sulpirid, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, sertralin, and tofisopam. Lithium is considered a child concept of antidepressants, reflecting its close pharmacological relationship to mood‑modifying therapies. The drugs bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride are also placed in the antidepressant therapeutic class. Both forms of bupropion are contraindicated in patients who have the withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الانسحاب), indicating that they should not be used in this clinical context. Conversely, tofisopam is noted as a medication that may be used to treat withdrawal syndrome, suggesting a potential therapeutic role for managing this condition. Together, these relationships delineate the categorization of antidepressant agents, their interconnections with related compounds such as lithium, and important safety considerations regarding contraindications and possible treatment applications for withdrawal syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: Aniracetam is classified in the ATC subgroup “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” and is recognized both as a nootropic and as an antidepressant, illustrating the overlap between cognitive‑enhancing and mood‑modulating agents. Bupropion, together with its salt forms bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride, is listed as an antidepressant and a smoking‑cessation agent, but it is contraindicated in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome; in this context the anxiolytic tofisopam may be employed to manage withdrawal symptoms. Other antidepressants in the “Mental health.behavior” psychoanaleptic category include citalopram, lithium, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride, esketamine, atomoxetine, trazodone, the broader “psychotropní léky” group, and the sodium salt of valproic acid as a mood stabilizer. Additional agents considered children of the antidepressant class are deanol, fipexide, klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolipram, tofisofam, and the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 271
retrieved_docs.content: Bupropion, in its hydrochloride and hydrobromide salt forms, is classified both as a smoking‑cessation agent and as a child drug of the antidepressant therapeutic class, reflecting its dual role in nicotine‑dependence treatment and mood regulation. The antidepressant class encompasses a range of specific agents, including desvenlafaxine succinate, esketamine, femoxetine, klorgylin, moklobemide, nialamid, pizotylin, rolipram, tofisopam, and trazodone, all of which are recognized as members of this pharmacologic group. Although lithium is primarily a mood stabilizer, it is frequently discussed in relation to antidepressant therapy because of its adjunctive use in depressive disorders, and trazodone is correctly placed as a specific antidepressant within the broader class. Critical drug‑drug and drug‑disease contraindications are highlighted in the network of relationships. Serotonergic antidepressants such as desvenlafaxine succinate and trazodone are contraindicated when co‑administered with the MAO‑inhibiting antibiotic linezolid due to the risk of serotonin syndrome. Bupropion (both hydrochloride and hydrobromide formulations) is contraindicated in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome, underscoring the need for careful assessment of dependence‑related conditions before initiating therapy. Conversely, tofisopam may be employed to treat withdrawal syndrome, suggesting a therapeutic role for this anxiolytic agent in managing withdrawal‑related symptoms. Overall, the community’s knowledge emphasizes the classification of numerous agents within the antidepressant class, the special status of bupropion as both a smoking‑cessation aid and an antidepressant, the proper placement of trazodone, and the essential drug‑disease and drug‑drug contraindications that must be considered to ensure safe and effective patient care.
